source.summary: Telemetry query timeouts triggered by incident-bot bursts in #incidents (v2 context cards) causing slow/empty diagnostics
source.body:
Issue Summary
During several customer-impacting events and internal incident drills, incident-bot v2 telemetry context cards intermittently rendered as empty or partially complete. Slack threads showed repeated “Telemetry query timed out” messages. Investigation indicates the bot can generate bursty query patterns (multiple responders + retries + interactive actions), which saturates the telemetry query service and triggers timeouts. These timeouts are most acute under incident conditions (elevated error rates + higher on-call activity), which is exactly when the bot is relied upon.

Impact
- On-call responders lost fast access to saturation signals (GPU util / queue depth / KV cache pressure), increasing time-to-triage.
- Telemetry query service experienced elevated p95/p99 latency; in worst cases, query workers hit concurrency limits and began failing fast.
- Secondary impact: dashboards and ad-hoc queries in the same time window degraded (shared query infrastructure).

Environment
- Production (primarily us-east, eu-west)
- Affects Slack channels: #incidents, #eng-oncall
- Affected service(s): telemetry-query-service (internal), incident-bot (Slack app)

Customer / Data sensitivity
Internal issue. No evidence of customer data exposure; failures were timeouts/empty cards only.

What we observed
- Bot posts initial incident context, then multiple responders click “Refresh signals” / “Expand details” within a short window.
- Slack interactive action retries (due to ack deadline + transient network latency) caused duplicate calls.
- Bot performed per-card, per-signal queries with limited coalescing; same incident/thread triggers repeated identical queries.

Symptoms (from bot / logs)
- Slack card banner: “Saturation signals unavailable (telemetry query timed out). Showing dashboard links.”
- telemetry-query-service logs: request_id spikes and increased “deadline exceeded” counts
- p99 latency spikes correlate with incident start + the first 10 minutes of heavy responder traffic

Steps to reproduce (reliable in staging with load)
1) Start a simulated incident thread and trigger bot context cards.
2) Have 5–10 users click “Refresh” and “Expand” within 60 seconds.
3) Observe telemetry query latency and bot timeouts, particularly if the telemetry cluster is already under moderate load.

Investigation notes
- Primary driver is burst concurrency and lack of request coalescing at the telemetry-query-service boundary.
- The bot also issues some high-cost queries (wide time windows + high-cardinality labels) when the incident metadata is incomplete (missing route/service scoping), causing fallback to broader queries.
- Slack retry behavior: interactive actions must ACK quickly; when upstream query exceeds budget, the bot may retry, amplifying bursts.

Mitigation / Fix
Implemented a multi-layer approach:
1) Cache + request coalescing for telemetry queries used by incident-bot
   - Short TTL caching (target 30–120s depending on signal class)
   - Per-(incident_id, region, service, route) de-duplication to collapse identical queries
   - Serve stale-on-error during incident windows (prefer last-known-good + links)

2) Rate limits + circuit breakers on diagnostics endpoints
   - Token bucket per bot identity + per incident thread
   - Hard cap on concurrent in-flight queries per region
   - Circuit breaker: when telemetry p95 exceeds threshold, bot switches to degraded mode automatically (links + cached summaries)

3) Tighter query budgets + bounded time windows
   - Default windows reduced for “first 5 minutes” cards
   - If incident metadata lacks scoping, bot displays an action to “Select service/region” instead of fanning out

4) Perf canary for telemetry query latency (bot-critical queries)
   - Synthetic queries run continuously
   - Alerts on sustained p95/p99 regression and timeout rate

Resolution
- Rolled out caching + timeout handling improvements in incident-bot.
- Added server-side rate limits/circuit breakers in diagnostics endpoints.
- Deployed telemetry query latency perf canary and alerting.
- Verified during two internal incident simulations and one production incident: bot returned cached summaries quickly and did not amplify telemetry load.

Verification
- Success criteria:
  - Bot context card render within 3s p95 (degraded mode allowed) in prod during load tests
  - Telemetry query timeout rate < 1% for bot-critical query set (sustained)
  - No >2x increase in telemetry-query-service QPS attributable to bot refresh bursts
- Observed (post-change):
  - Bot “saturation signals” card returns within ~1–2s when cached, ~2–4s uncached; falls back to links after budget expires
  - Telemetry query p99 no longer spikes linearly with Slack action bursts

Follow-ups / Action Items
- Document query budget and allowed label sets for incident-bot (owner: Allison Grant)
- Add guardrail test to prevent introducing high-cardinality dimensions into bot queries (owner: Nadia Rahman)
- Confirm bot’s Slack retries are idempotent across all interactive actions (owner: Devon Price)
- Update on-call troubleshooting playbook for empty cards to reflect degraded-mode behavior and where to check perf canary (owner: Rafael Mendes)

Comments
2025-02-18 (Miles Thornton): Creating ticket after multiple reports that incident-bot v2 saturation cards are timing out during bursts in #incidents. We need a fix before expanding beta beyond SRE rotation.

2025-02-18 (Logan Wright): Looking at telemetry-query-service. Seeing concurrency spikes + deadline exceeded clustered around bot activity. Suspect missing cache/coalescing. Will propose TTL caching and a per-thread rate limit.

2025-02-19 (Jacob Stein): From bot logs, Slack interactive actions retried when ack arrives late. We’re not fully idempotent on refresh path; duplicates are generating identical telemetry queries.

2025-02-20 (Nadia Rahman): Confirmed some queries are unbounded when route/service labels missing. Recommend forcing scoping in bot UI or using safe defaults with narrow windows.

2025-02-24 (Logan Wright): Implemented server-side throttles + circuit breaker config in diagnostics endpoints. Initial load test: QPS is capped; bot falls back to cached context + links.

2025-03-03 (Aisha Bello): Adding perf canary to catch regressions earlier; alert will page obs-oncall only (not #incidents) to avoid noise.

2025-03-06 (Miles Thornton): Verified during drill. Cards load reliably; when telemetry is slow, we get cached summary and deep links. Closing as Resolved.

source.summary: Private deployments: request audit log export to customer-owned object storage (S3/GCS)
source.description:
Issue summary
- Multiple Private prospects/customers are blocked in security review until we can export audit logs to customer-managed storage.

Impact
- Deals at risk: at least 2 (see SUP-2194 for one active account).
- Current Private audit logs are only accessible via in-cluster tooling and do not meet customer retention requirements.

Requested outcome
- Support export of audit logs to customer-owned object storage.
- Minimum: AWS S3 and/or GCS.
- Requirements noted by customers: encryption at rest, least-privilege auth, predictable prefixes, delivery gap detection.

Environment
- Private (customer-managed Kubernetes), generally EKS and GKE.

Notes
- Solutions requests Azure Blob support from one customer; not required for initial unblock but may come up.

Comments
2025-01-21 Ava Chen (reporter): Customer security team asked for "ship audit logs to our S3 bucket" + proof we don't use long-lived credentials. Can we commit to a timeline?

2025-01-22 Diego Fernandez: We'll draft design in Confluence and align with Security on requirements. Target: prototype in ~2 weeks.

2025-01-28 Aisha Rahman (Security): Non-negotiable: no static keys; use AssumeRole/WIF only. Also need clear evidence doc.

source.summary: Add SDK guardrail: detect 'Bearer ' prefix in api_key and warn across SDKs
source.description: Background: Support saw multiple customer-reported auth issues due to key/header formatting across SDKs (SUP-4821, SUP-4884, SUP-4902, SUP-4933). Proposal: add a shared validation behavior across SDKs: (1) if api_key value begins with 'Bearer ' strip prefix and log warning (2) if trailing CR/LF exists, trim and warn (3) if other whitespace exists, error with actionable message. Also update docs and support playbook.
comments: Logan Wright: Align behavior w/ TS and Go changes already merged; need python follow-up PR for Bearer prefix detection.
Ava Chen: Please prioritize docs update; support keeps seeing this.
Logan Wright: Will draft minimal spec and open PRs per SDK.

source.summary: Provision dedicated GPU benchmark runner capacity for Runtime LTS perf gates (avoid blocking CI)
source.description:
Request
- We need dedicated GPU runner capacity for perf-canary benchmarks used as *release gates* for Runtime LTS patch releases.
- Current state: perf jobs are contending with general CI and ad-hoc benchmark runs, causing queue times and occasional partial coverage during patch week.

Why this matters
- LTS patches are blocked on perf gate results (p50/p95 latency + throughput across canonical workloads). Any runner starvation delays releases and increases risk of releasing without full coverage.
- We also need to avoid impacting regular developer CI and nightly runs.

Scope
- Provision/allocate a separate runner pool (or reserved quota) for perf-canary “release-branch / LTS gate” workflows.
- Ensure capacity exists for concurrent runs during patch week (RC validation + reruns for flakes) without starving mainline.

Requirements (from Runtime LTS workstream)
- GPU SKUs: H100 (primary), A100 (secondary) at minimum.
- Concurrency target: 6 parallel benchmark jobs (H100) + 2 parallel (A100) during patch week; outside patch week, allow scale down to 2+1.
- Runtime: each job ~45–70 minutes wall time; expect 2–3 full gate cycles per RC (initial run + rerun(s)).
- Determinism: pin driver/container image; avoid noisy neighbors (dedicated nodes preferred).

Acceptance criteria
- New runner pool available to perf-canary with labels (e.g., runner_group=lts-perf-gate).
- perf-canary workflows updated to target that pool for release/* and runtime-*-lts branches.
- Documented scheduling policy + escalation path if capacity is exhausted.

Owners / stakeholders
- Requestor: Sahana Iyer (CI/build)
- Release Eng: Connor O’Brien
- Runtime perf: Marcus Reed / Jonas Weber
- SRE: Sean Gallagher (assignee), Rafael Mendes (backup)

References
- Slack: #eng-sre / “lts-perf-gate-thresholds” and #eng-infra / runner capacity discussion
- Related: INT-3302 for release-branch CI permissions/secrets; ENG-4825 for perf gate integration into LTS release.
comments:
2025-02-04 10:12 PST — Sahana Iyer (Reporter)
We’re at risk of blocking the first 1.18.x LTS RC because perf-canary is waiting behind general GPU CI. Can we carve out a small reserved pool for LTS gates? Even 4x H100 reserved during patch week would help; we’re seeing 2–4 hour queue times.

2025-02-04 11:03 PST — Sean Gallagher (Assignee)
Ack. Need specifics:
1) Which workflows/branches should use reserved pool?
2) Minimum SKU coverage required for “ship/no-ship”?
3) Expected utilization so we can justify reservation.
Also: do we need bare-metal dedicated or OK with k8s node pool + taints?

2025-02-04 11:27 PST — Connor O’Brien (Release Eng)
Target only:
- release/* branches and runtime-*-lts branches
- tagged RC runs
Ship/no-ship = H100 required; A100 is “recommended” but can be waived w/ release manager sign-off (documented in LTS runbook). Preference for dedicated nodes or strong isolation; we’ve seen noisy neighbor variance affect p95 thresholds.

2025-02-05 09:18 PST — Marcus Reed (Perf suite owner)
Benchmarks are sensitive to contention. For LTS gates, we should isolate GPU + CPU. k8s node pool with taints is fine if nodes are not shared with general CI and we pin the image/driver. Also need stable clocking (no auto power mgmt surprises).

2025-02-06 14:02 PST — Rafael Mendes (Escalation/on-call manager)
From on-call perspective: we need an escalation knob. Suggest: reserved pool with max queue length; if queued >45m during patch week, page #eng-sre-oncall with this ticket. That prevents release week deadlocks.

2025-02-07 16:40 PST — Sean Gallagher (Assignee)
Plan proposal:
- Create ‘gpu-perf-lts’ runner group in us-east with:
  - 6x H100 nodes (dedicated), 2x A100 nodes (dedicated)
  - taints to prevent general CI scheduling
  - runner labels: lts-perf-gate, sku=h100/a100
- Time-based autoscaling policy:
  - Patch week (Mon–Thu): keep full capacity warm
  - Off-cycle: min 2x H100, 1x A100
- Update perf-canary workflow selector for release/* and runtime-*-lts.
Pending: cost approval from Infra budget owner + confirmation on patch week calendar.

2025-02-10 10:15 PST — Sahana Iyer
Works. Patch week cadence is “first full week of each month”, with out-of-band security patches as needed. Connor can share calendar invites. Also note: perf-canary PR-418 is ready; it just needs runner labels to target.

2025-02-11 13:55 PST — Jonas Weber (Perf gates advisor)
Please ensure we can run reruns without waiting for scale-up. Perf gate flakes happen; we typically need 1 rerun ~30% of the time. Keeping warm capacity during patch week is important.

2025-02-13 09:22 PST — Sean Gallagher
Implemented runner group + taints. H100 pool live; A100 nodes still provisioning (cloud capacity delay). Wired perf-canary release-branch workflow to use lts-perf-gate label.
Remaining: A100 ready + doc update + add Grafana panel for runner queue time so we can see contention early.

2025-02-14 17:08 PST — Sahana Iyer
Validated: release/* jobs are now scheduling immediately on H100. Queue time dropped from hours to <5 minutes. Thanks. Please keep this stable through LTS pilot.

2025-02-18 15:31 PST — Sean Gallagher
A100 pool now live. Added runner queue time panel + alert: queue_wait_p95 > 45m during patch week => page SRE on-call. Doc’ed escalation in internal runbook notes (linked from LTS patch runbook). Marking resolved.
source.resolution: Provisioned dedicated GPU runner pool for LTS perf gates (6x H100 + 2x A100 in us-east), isolated from general CI via taints/labels. Updated perf-canary workflows to target lts-perf-gate runners for release/* and runtime-*-lts branches. Added monitoring/alerting for queue delays with SRE on-call escalation during patch week.
follow_ups: Add eu-west capacity for LTS perf gates (H100) to avoid regional dependency (new INT ticket TBD).
Document monthly patch-week calendar and cost reporting owner in Release Eng runbook.
Revisit autoscaling mins after 2 patch cycles; adjust based on observed utilization and flake rerun rates.

source.summary: Enable Hosted data residency enforcement feature flag in staging + seed test orgs/API keys for verification
source.description:
Background
We are preparing end-to-end staging verification for Hosted data residency enforcement (region pinning + deterministic blocks + standardized errors). This internal support request is to enable the staging feature flags and seed a few test orgs/projects/API keys so Engineering + SRE + Security can validate routing, fallback behavior, logging boundaries, and the public error contract.

Request
1) Enable residency enforcement feature flags in staging
- Enable residency policy evaluation + enforcement in gateway + routing.
- Ensure cross-region fallback is disabled by default when residency constraints are present.

2) Seed staging test entities
Create the following in staging control plane (do not use real customer data):
A) Org: "residency-e2e-eu-only" (owner: platform-test@redwood.example)
- Project: "hosted-chat"
- Residency policy: allowed_regions=[EU], primary_region=EU (EU region group as defined in internal mapping)
- API key(s):
  - Key 1: project-scoped (standard)
  - Key 2: key-level override (optional) to validate precedence rules

B) Org: "residency-e2e-us-only"
- Project: "hosted-chat"
- Residency policy: allowed_regions=[US], primary_region=US
- API key: project-scoped

C) Org: "residency-e2e-unpinned"
- Project: "hosted-chat"
- No residency policy (control behavior baseline)

Notes / constraints
- Do not paste full API keys into this ticket. Store in 1Password shared vault "Staging Test Credentials" under item: "INT-9312 residency e2e keys".
- Ensure audit log emission is enabled for policy create/update/delete in staging (for evidence + UI troubleshooting).
- Ensure request logs for blocked requests include residency decision context fields (policy_id, evaluated_allowed_regions, selected_region, block_reason, request_id) without logging prompt/content.

Approvals needed
- Security Engineering sign-off for staging enablement: Marcus Lin
- Product owner sign-off for staging test plan: Naomi Feldman

Success criteria
- Staging environment can deterministically reproduce a residency block and returns the standardized error contract for both streaming and non-streaming endpoints.
- Routing does not attempt cross-region fallback when policy prohibits it.
- Console shows the seeded policies and recent blocked requests, and audit log shows config changes.

References
- Slack: #eng-sre (thread on alerting + enablement) and #eng-platform (schema/policy precedence)
- Planned enforcement/error contract PRs: PR-21507, PR-21533
- Related support bug being validated in staging: SUP-28577 (streaming returned generic 500 previously)
source.validation_checklist:
Pre-flight
- [ ] Confirm staging gateway + routing builds include latest residency enforcement changes.
- [ ] Confirm residency policy service is deployed in staging and reachable from gateway/routing.
- [ ] Confirm feature flags exist in staging config service and are scoped to staging only.

Enablement
- [ ] Enable flag: residency_enforcement_enabled=true (staging)
- [ ] Enable flag: residency_decision_logging_enabled=true (staging)
- [ ] Ensure cross-region fallback default behavior: disabled when residency policy present

Seed data
- [ ] Create org/project + EU-only policy + API keys (stored in 1Password)
- [ ] Create org/project + US-only policy + API key (stored in 1Password)
- [ ] Create org/project with no policy (baseline)
- [ ] Verify policies visible in Console and config history entries recorded

Functional verification
- [ ] EU-only org: send request via us-east endpoint -> request is blocked with standardized error payload (409) and error_code indicates residency violation
- [ ] EU-only org: send request via eu endpoint -> succeeds and selected_region is EU
- [ ] Streaming path: verify blocked request returns residency error (not generic 500) and includes request_id
- [ ] Unpinned org: verify existing routing behavior unchanged

Observability
- [ ] Residency decision context present in logs for allow + block (no prompt/content)
- [ ] Audit log entries exist for policy create/update/delete
- [ ] Dashboard panels populate for blocked_requests count in staging (if hooked up)

Rollback
- [ ] Confirm flags can be toggled off and behavior reverts without redeploy
comments: 2025-02-10 10:14 - Sean Gallagher (Assignee): Acknowledged. Will coordinate flag enablement after confirming current staging deploy contains gateway PR-21533 build. Will also confirm routing has PR-21507 in staging before flipping enforcement on.

2025-02-10 14:02 - Amara Diallo (API Gateway): Gateway staging is on build from main @ 7c31d2a; includes residency error contract changes behind flag. Streaming path fix for SUP-28577 is included. OK to proceed once routing is confirmed.

2025-02-11 09:18 - Mina Haddad (Routing): Routing staging includes residency evaluation integration (PR-21507) behind the same flag. Cross-region fallback is disabled by default when residency policy present. Only exception is explicitly configured allow_cross_region_fallback (not set for these test orgs).

2025-02-11 11:27 - Marcus Lin (Security Engineering): Approved staging enablement with constraints: do not log prompts/outputs; ensure policy_id is OK to expose internally; confirm audit events are present for config changes. Please capture screenshots/links for evidence pack once validated.
2025-02-11 13:05 - Sean Gallagher (SRE): Flags enabled in staging at 13:02 UTC. Seed requests in progress. Created 1Password item and shared with #eng-platform + #eng-sre. (No keys pasted here.)

2025-02-11 16:44 - Chloe Martin (Console): Confirmed Console shows all three orgs and policy history entries. Blocked requests view shows entries after first test run; request_id links to logs in staging Kibana.

2025-02-12 10:21 - Brian Murphy (API error contract): Verified block response: HTTP 409, error_code=RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION, includes request_id and help_url. Streaming path now returns same structured error (previously generic 500).
2025-02-13 09:03 - Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager): Marking resolved. Next: add staging monitor to catch unexpected spike in block rate during dogfood (tracked separately by ENG-4846 dashboards).
source.resolution: Completed. Residency enforcement flags enabled in staging; seeded EU-only, US-only, and unpinned test orgs/projects/keys (stored in 1Password). Validated standardized residency violation errors (409) for both streaming and non-streaming, confirmed no cross-region fallback under residency policy, Console visibility + audit log events present.

source.summary: Break-glass access request audit — time-bound elevation for Private control plane role binding fix (Northbridge POC)
source.description:
Request type: Break-glass (emergency admin elevation)

Requester: Colin O'Donnell (Solutions Eng, Private customer pilot lead)
Requested by (ticket reporter): Fiona McCarthy (SecOps)
System(s): Redwood Private Control Plane (customer VPC), RBAC v2 enabled (pilot)

source.summary: Staging: Private control-plane backup CronJob failing with RBAC "forbidden" (cannot list/get secrets + Redwood CRs)
source.description:
Issue summary
- The scheduled backup CronJob deployed via Helm in staging is failing immediately due to missing RBAC permissions.
- This blocks our nightly backup verification and is causing noise for on-call.

Impact
- Staging backup schedule is effectively disabled (no successful backups since enabling the chart values).
- No customer impact (staging only), but this blocks Day-2 ops hardening and restore drill automation.

Environment
- Cluster: private-staging-us-east-1
- Namespace: redwood-private
- Chart: redwood-private (backup CronJob enabled)
- Backup storage backend: S3 (staging bucket)

Symptoms
- CronJob pods crash with "forbidden" errors when attempting to enumerate resources required for control-plane backup.

Steps to reproduce
1) Deploy/upgrade Helm release with backup enabled:
   - values.backup.enabled=true
   - values.backup.schedule="0 2 * * *"
2) Wait for CronJob to create a Job.
3) Inspect logs for the Job pod:
   kubectl -n redwood-private logs job/<job-name>

Observed logs (redacted)
- E0122 02:01:14.123] error: failed preflight: unable to read cluster state: secrets is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:redwood-private:redwood-backup" cannot list resource "secrets" in API group "" in the namespace "redwood-private"
- E0122 02:01:14.124] error: failed inventory: redwoodtenants.platform.redwood.ai is forbidden: User "system:serviceaccount:redwood-private:redwood-backup" cannot list resource "redwoodtenants" in API group "platform.redwood.ai" in the namespace "redwood-private"

Expected behavior
- Backup CronJob service account should have the minimal RBAC required to read backup inputs (config/state CRs, configmaps, select secrets or secret references) and create the backup artifact.

Notes
- This appears to be a Helm chart RBAC template gap (service account is created, but Role/RoleBinding is incomplete/missing required verbs/resources when backup is enabled).
comments:
2025-01-22 02:18 (Sean Gallagher): Paging noise started after enabling scheduled backups in staging. Job pods failing with RBAC forbidden. Attaching command output:
- kubectl -n redwood-private get pods | grep backup
- kubectl -n redwood-private logs job/redwood-private-backup-28947281

2025-01-22 03:02 (Sean Gallagher): Confirmed serviceAccountName on the pod is "redwood-backup". RoleBinding exists but Role only includes configmaps. Looks like it never got secrets + Redwood CRDs.

2025-01-22 09:41 (Bruno Silva): Taking this. I suspect we templated RBAC for configmaps only and missed the CRDs used by the backup inventory step. Will diff staging rendered templates vs latest chart and propose minimal RBAC.

2025-01-22 12:10 (Bruno Silva): Root cause: RBAC template for backup CronJob uses a Role that does not include:
- core/secrets (needed for reading the backup config secret + optional S3 credentials in staging)
- platform.redwood.ai/* custom resources (redwoodtenants, routingpolicies) that the backup CLI inventories for a consistent snapshot
Also RoleBinding was conditional on .Values.backup.rbac.create but our staging values only set backup.enabled.

2025-01-22 12:23 (Bruno Silva): Proposed fix (staging hotfix):
1) Set values.backup.rbac.create=true
2) Expand Role rules to include:
- apiGroups: [""] resources: ["configmaps","secrets"] verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
- apiGroups: ["platform.redwood.ai"] resources: ["redwoodtenants","routingpolicies"] verbs: ["get","list","watch"]
- apiGroups: ["batch"] resources: ["jobs"] verbs: ["get","list"] (for status reporting)

2025-01-22 13:05 (Sean Gallagher): Applied temporary patch by manually creating Role/RoleBinding (see below). Next scheduled run succeeded.
Manual patch (staging only):
- kubectl apply -f rbac-redwood-backup.yaml
- verified with: kubectl auth can-i list secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:redwood-private:redwood-backup -n redwood-private

2025-01-23 10:18 (Bruno Silva): Opened/update in helm charts (PR noted in ticket). Added:
- sensible defaults: when backup.enabled, also create rbac by default
- tightened permissions to namespace-scoped Role (not ClusterRole)
- docs note: if your deployment uses external-secrets, add read-only to those CRs (optional)

2025-01-24 08:27 (Sean Gallagher): Pulled updated chart into staging, removed manual RBAC objects, confirmed CronJob succeeds. Marking resolved.
source.resolution: Resolved by updating the Helm chart to create the backup Role/RoleBinding when backups are enabled and to include the minimal namespace-scoped permissions required by the backup CLI (core configmaps/secrets + Redwood platform CRs). Staging was unblocked by a temporary manual RBAC patch while the chart fix was rolled out.
source.workaround: Staging-only workaround prior to chart fix: create a namespace Role/RoleBinding granting the backup service account get/list/watch on configmaps + required Redwood CRs, plus get/list secrets if S3 creds are stored as a secret. Verify with `kubectl auth can-i` before re-running the CronJob.
follow_up_tasks: Add a Helm template test (or CI render check) that asserts backup.enabled implies creation of ServiceAccount+Role+RoleBinding with expected rules.
Update the internal runbook to include an RBAC preflight section: `kubectl auth can-i` checks for the backup SA.
Confirm backup process does not require reading secret payloads beyond explicit backup config/credentials (align with security decision: prefer secret references; avoid backing up secret material).
Add an SRE alert in staging for CronJob consecutive failures > 2 runs (non-paging during maintenance window).

source.summary: Office whiteboard marker refill station request (misc ops)
source.description: Issue summary: Kitchen/courtyard whiteboard markers are nearly depleted and there is no refill station. Impact: Teams in 3rd-floor courtyard and adjacent meeting rooms are frequently out of markers during brainstorming sessions. Request: Install a small magnetic marker holder with an initial stock of 12 assorted dry-erase markers and 2 erasers near the courtyard whiteboard. Steps to reproduce/observe: Walk to 3rd-floor courtyard whiteboard after 2pm, typically only 1 marker remains. Preferred vendor: office-supplies-standard SKU (if available). Budget: <$50. Notes: This is a low-priority facilities request but helps avoid frequent interruptions to meetings. If procurement prefers bulk purchase for multiple floors, advise and I'll coordinate a consolidated request.

source.summary: Wi-Fi EAP-TLS certificate rotation fails after imaging, blocking SCCM/Intune policy application
source.description: Issue summary: After new-image provisioning via SCCM/Autopilot + Intune, fleet devices intermittently fail to connect to corporate Wi-Fi. Impact: users cannot reach internal services until manual remediation; affects small percentage of newly imaged devices in US-east. Root cause suspected during certificate rotation flow: machine certificate is not present in the local machine store at time Wi‑Fi profile with EAP-TLS is applied, causing profile application to fail and network location services to stall.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Image Dell Latitude 7440 with current SCCM TS that installs Intune agent, applies SCEP cert enrollment, and deploys Wi-Fi profile
2) Allow Intune device configuration to run (device config + Wi-Fi profile targeted),
3) Observe that Wi-Fi profile fails to apply and Windows shows 'Cannot connect to this network' for corp-802.1X
4) Check local machine certificate store and Intune logs to confirm certificate missing or enrollment pending.
logs: Event Viewer/System: Network Location Awareness (NlaSvc) delayed start - Service hung waiting for certificate (EventID 7009)
Intune Management Extension log: 'WiFiProfile: Failed to configure profile corp-802.1X - Required certificate not found'
SCEP event: 'Enrollment pending - CA responded with RA pending' on first attempt, succeeded on re-enroll
SCCM smsts.log during TS: Intune client registration completed before SCEP refresh, timing mismatch observed
investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: timing/race between Intune SCEP certificate enrollment and Wi-Fi profile deployment (profile applied too early).
Reproduced in lab by accelerating Intune policy application: when Wi-Fi policy applied within first 2 minutes after Intune client install, profile fails 85% of runs.
Observed tokens: SCEP uses device challenge which sometimes requires CA RA step we do not control; CA latency correlates with failure windows.
Temporary workaround validated: re-run 'Sync' from Company Portal or trigger 'Device Configuration' in Intune, profile then applies successfully once certificate present.
SCCM task sequence earlier version performed an explicit 'Wait for Intune' script; recent TS refactor removed that wait to speed provisioning—introduced the race.
comments: Priya Natarajan (Reporter) - 2025-01-12: Customer-facing devices in US-east imaged today showed ~6% failure rate. Ticket raised to capture recurring cases and find remediation.
Omar Velasquez (Assignee) - 2025-01-12: Reproduced in lab. Adding a conditional wait in TS and creating repro steps. Filing for change to provisioning TS.
SRE (Dana Liu) - 2025-01-13: Checked CA RA logs; there are intermittent 2-5s spikes, but not enough to explain 60-90s gaps. Focus on orchestration ordering in TS + Intune timing.

Infra (Marcus Hale) - 2025-01-13: Confirmed Intune policy push can happen before SCEP enrollment completes. Suggest marking Wi-Fi profile to require certificate present (if supported) or delay application.
source.resolution: Root cause: race condition introduced by a TS refactor that removed an explicit wait for Intune/SCEP completion. Fix implemented: (1) rollback of the TS change to reintroduce a controlled wait until certificate enrollment status is confirmed, (2) added a transient device-group-targeting flag in Intune to delay Wi-Fi profile deployment for newly imaged devices, (3) updated SCCM TS to trigger a post-TS 'Intune device sync' and verify certificate presence before applying network profiles. Deployed change to US-east pilot pool (50 devices) on 2025-01-13 — success: failure rate dropped to 0%. Follow-up: file to automate SCEP enrollment checks in TS and add monitoring alert if Wi-Fi policy application error rate >1% for new devices. Documented runbook and scheduled audit of other TS refactors.

title: Wi-Fi EAP-TLS certificate rotation fails after imaging, blocking SCCM/Intune policy application
preview: Issue summary: After new-image provisioning via SCCM/Autopilot + Intune, fleet devices intermittently fail to connect to corporate Wi-Fi. Impact: users cannot reach internal services until manual remediation; affects small percentage of newly imaged devices in US-east. Root cause suspected during certificate rotation flow: machine certificate is not present in the local machine store at time Wi‑Fi profile with EAP-TLS is applied, causing profile application to fail and network location services to stall.
text: Issue summary: After new-image provisioning via SCCM/Autopilot + Intune, fleet devices intermittently fail to connect to corporate Wi-Fi.
